skill.name: discussion-section-architect
skill.description: Structures and writes discussion sections for academic papers and research reports. Use when writing a discussion section, interpreting research results, connecting findings to existing literature, addressing study limitations, synthesizing conclusions, or drafting any part of an academic discussion. Helps researchers organize arguments, contextualize data, and produce clear, publication-ready discussion prose.
skill.summary: AI-powered framework for structuring and writing academic discussion sections for research papers and manuscripts.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 56 static analysis findings were evaluated and confirmed as false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections matched common words containing hash-like substrings in documentation strings and dict method calls. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' detections were all markdown inline code formatting in documentation, not actual shell execution. The 'certificate/key files' detection was the Python .keys() dict method. The 'hardcoded URL' detection was a documentation hyperlink to the source GitHub repository with no corresponding network code. The 'system reconnaissance' detections matched documentation words like 'scope' and 'execution' in audit result notes. The heuristic combination warnings were triggered by aggregating these false positive signals. The only real operational pattern is a controlled file write at scripts/main.py:149 that writes templated discussion outlines to user-specified paths, which is the intended functionality of this academic writing tool. No prompt injection attempts, data exfiltration, credential theft, or malicious code patterns were found.

low_findings.title: Controlled File Write via User-Specified Path
low_findings.description: The script at scripts/main.py:149 opens a file for writing using a user-provided output path from command-line arguments. The content written is a templated discussion section outline generated by the skill, not executable code. This is legitimate functionality for an academic writing tool that needs to save generated outlines. No path traversal or injection risk was identified as the output path is explicitly provided by the user running the script locally.

content.user_title: Write Academic Discussion Sections with AI
content.value_statement: Writing discussion sections for research papers is difficult and time-consuming. This skill provides a structured framework that organizes findings, connects to literature, addresses limitations, and produces publication-ready discussion prose.
actual_capabilities: Generate structured discussion section outlines with six key subsections including opening, interpretation, and conclusions
Interpret research results and connect findings to existing scientific literature with appropriate hedged language
Draft honest limitations sections that acknowledge constraints without undermining the research contribution
Provide transition phrases between discussion subsections for smooth academic prose flow
Guide an iterative draft-revise-check workflow with a built-in quality checklist
Synthesize conclusions that restate core findings and tie back to original research questions
limitations: Cannot access or search external literature databases for real citations and references
Does not perform statistical analysis or verify the accuracy of research data provided by the user
Output quality depends entirely on the accuracy and completeness of user-provided inputs and findings
Cannot verify the factual correctness of scientific claims or ensure compliance with specific journal formatting requirements

use_cases.title: Early-Career Researcher Drafting a First Manuscript
use_cases.description: Graduate students and postdocs use the skill to learn proper discussion section structure and produce a well-organized first draft for their initial publications.

use_cases.title: Experienced Academic Streamlining Writing Workflow
use_cases.description: Senior researchers use the skill to quickly generate structured outlines and focus their effort on refining scientific arguments rather than organizing content from scratch.

use_cases.title: Research Writing Instructor Teaching Structure
use_cases.description: Academic writing instructors use the skill to demonstrate proper discussion section organization and teach students the components of effective scientific argumentation.
use_cases.target_user: Academic writing instructors and research mentors

prompt_templates.title: Basic Discussion Section Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: I have research results for a study on [topic]. The key findings are: [list findings]. My hypothesis was: [state hypothesis]. Please generate a complete discussion section following the standard academic structure.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user needs a first draft of a discussion section from raw research findings

prompt_templates.title: Literature Comparison and Contextualization
prompt_templates.prompt: My study found that [key finding]. Prior work by [Author (Year)] reported [related finding]. Please draft a comparison paragraph that explains how my results align with or differ from the existing literature, using appropriate academic hedging language.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher needs to position their findings within the existing body of scientific literature

prompt_templates.title: Limitations Section with Future Directions
prompt_templates.prompt: My study has the following limitations: [list constraints]. Please draft a limitations subsection that honestly acknowledges these issues while preserving the value of the contribution, and suggest specific future research directions for each limitation.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher needs to address study weaknesses transparently while maintaining manuscript strength

prompt_templates.title: Full Iterative Draft-Revise Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Here is my draft discussion section: [paste draft]. Please review it against this checklist: each finding is addressed, claims are supported, unexpected results are acknowledged, limitations are stated without undermining the study, no new data is introduced, hedged language is used appropriately, and the conclusion ties back to the research question. For each failed item, revise only the affected paragraph.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user performing systematic quality review and targeted revision of a discussion draft

output_examples.input: Results: Group A showed a 23% reduction in symptom severity (p=0.003) vs. control. Hypothesis: Intervention would reduce symptom severity.
output_examples.output: The 23% reduction in symptom severity (p=0.003) supports the primary hypothesis that the intervention would reduce symptom severity. This effect size is clinically meaningful and consistent with the mechanistic rationale proposed in the introduction. The magnitude of improvement exceeds the minimal clinically important difference established by prior work, suggesting the intervention has practical as well as statistical significance.

output_examples.input: Finding: Effect was stronger in older participants. Literature: Smith et al. (2019) found age-moderated responses in a similar cohort.
output_examples.output: The age-moderated effect observed in this study aligns with Smith et al. (2019), who reported attenuated responses in younger adults within a similar cohort. One possible explanation is differential receptor sensitivity across age groups, as suggested by the pharmacokinetic modeling in Jones et al. (2021). However, the present study extends these findings by demonstrating that the age interaction persists even after controlling for baseline severity, which was not addressed in earlier work.

output_examples.input: Limitation: Small sample size (n=45), single-center design.
output_examples.output: Several limitations of this study should be noted. First, the sample size of 45 participants, while adequately powered for the primary analysis, limits the generalizability of subgroup comparisons. Second, the single-center design may constrain the applicability of findings to broader populations with different demographic characteristics. Future multi-center studies with larger, more diverse cohorts would strengthen confidence in these results. Despite these constraints, the robust effect size and consistent internal replication across sensitivity analyses support the reliability of the primary conclusions.

best_practices: Always provide complete research findings, hypothesis, and relevant literature context before generating a discussion section to ensure coherent output
Use the draft-revise loop to iteratively improve each subsection rather than accepting the first generated draft
Review all AI-generated scientific claims and interpretations against your actual data before including them in a manuscript submission
anti_patterns: Do not submit the AI-generated discussion as a final manuscript without thorough human review and editing for accuracy and voice
Do not rely on the skill to generate or fabricate citations and literature references that you have not verified yourself
Do not input confidential, unpublished, or patient-level research data without ensuring appropriate data security and compliance measures

faq.question: What types of academic papers does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports discussion sections for research papers across most academic disciplines including medical, scientific, social science, and engineering fields. The structure adapts to the standard IMRAD format used in peer-reviewed journals.

faq.question: Can this skill generate real citations and references?
faq.answer: No, this skill cannot search external databases or generate verified citations. You must provide the literature and references you want to cite. The skill helps you structure how to discuss and compare your findings with that literature.

faq.question: How is this different from asking Claude to write a discussion section directly?
faq.answer: This skill provides a structured framework with predefined subsections, transition phrases, and a draft-revise checklist. It enforces consistency and completeness that a free-form conversation may miss, and includes Python tooling for offline outline generation.

faq.question: Does the skill handle field-specific writing conventions?
faq.answer: The skill uses general academic writing conventions suitable for most fields. You should adapt the output to match your target journal's specific formatting, terminology, and style requirements.

faq.question: What inputs do I need to provide to get started?
faq.answer: You need to provide your research question, key findings with statistical results, your original hypothesis, and any prior literature you want to reference. The more complete your inputs, the better the generated discussion section.

faq.question: Can I customize the discussion section structure?
faq.answer: Yes, the included Python script supports selecting specific sections via the --sections flag. You can also modify the STRUCTURE_TEMPLATE in scripts/main.py to match your preferred organization.
